sources: ICC International Code Council adoption maps
American Electrical Co generator permit guide
_meta.disclaimer: Requirements vary by AHJ (Authority Having Jurisdiction). Always verify with the local building department. This knowledge base provides strong defaults — Tavily live search overrides these when city-specific data is available.

hvac.governing_code: International Mechanical Code (IMC) — most states. California uses Uniform Mechanical Code (UMC).

permit_required.rule: REQUIRED in nearly all US jurisdictions for any HVAC installation or replacement
always_required: New HVAC system installation
Full system replacement (air handler + condenser)
Furnace replacement
Ductwork modifications or extensions
Mini split installation (new refrigerant lines)
Gas line connections or modifications
Adding new HVAC zones
usually_required: Like-for-like condenser replacement (same tonnage, same location)
sometimes_exempt: Replacing a blower motor (no refrigerant work, no gas line work)
permit_required.key_rule: Any work involving refrigerant, gas lines, new electrical connections, or ductwork modifications requires a permit in all 50 states.

hvac.who_can_pull: Licensed mechanical or HVAC contractor required in most states. Some states allow licensed electricians to pull the electrical portion separately. Homeowners can pull in some states for owner-occupied single-family homes.

approval_timeline.over_the_counter: Same day in most suburban cities
approval_timeline.plan_review: 1–5 business days if plans required (new construction, complex systems)
approval_timeline.noted_slow_cities: Los Angeles (2–4 weeks), New York City (2–6 weeks), Chicago (1–2 weeks)

inspections.description: Inspector checks refrigerant lines, ductwork connections, electrical rough-in, and gas piping before walls are closed. Must be done before insulating or covering any work.
inspections.timing: Before covering any ductwork, refrigerant lines, or electrical connections

inspections.description: Inspector verifies system is fully operational, all connections are secure, system is properly sized, and all safety shutoffs are in place.
inspections.timing: After system is fully installed and operational

inspections.description: Separate inspection for any gas piping work. Pressure test required.
inspections.timing: Before gas is turned on, after rough-in
inspections.required_when: When gas lines are modified or new gas connections made

common_mistakes: Starting work before permit is issued — results in double permit fees in many jurisdictions
Not scheduling rough-in inspection before covering ductwork
Improper refrigerant line sizing or insulation
Missing disconnect switch within sight of outdoor unit
Electrical not pulled with mechanical permit — often needs separate electrical permit for new circuits
pro_tips: Pull the permit before ordering equipment — some cities require equipment specs on the application
Most cities allow over-the-counter permits for like-for-like replacements — call ahead and ask
In states with tight labor boards (CA, NY, IL), verify your license is active in that specific county
If adding a mini split, check if local utility requires separate interconnection approval

state_notes.CA: HVAC work requires C-20 license (Warm-Air Heating). All work under Title 24 energy code. Most jurisdictions require permit for any replacement.
state_notes.FL: Licensed CAC (Central Air Conditioning) contractor required statewide. Strong enforcement — FL is one of the most active states for unlicensed HVAC prosecution. HVAC permit required for all replacements.
state_notes.TX: HVAC work requires TACL (Texas Air Conditioning Contractor License) or HVAC-R license. Local jurisdictions control permits.
state_notes.NY: NYC requires Licensed Master Plumber for gas work and Licensed Electrician for electrical — separate permits often required for split systems. Upstate: varies by county, generally licensed HVAC contractor.
state_notes.IL: Chicago has its own permit system and strict licensed contractor requirements. Suburban Cook County follows state code. Downstate follows local jurisdictions. Licensed HVAC/Mechanical contractor required.
state_notes.AZ: ROC (Registrar of Contractors) license required — CR-67 (refrigeration). Permit requirements vary by city — Phoenix, Scottsdale, Tucson all have own permit offices.
state_notes.CO: Denver has home rule permit system. Boulder, Colorado Springs, Fort Collins all have own AHJs. Licensed HVAC contractor required in Denver.
state_notes.NV: State C-21 license (refrigeration). Clark County (Las Vegas) and City of Las Vegas have separate permit offices. Henderson uses Clark County.
state_notes.GA: Licensed HVAC contractor (unrestricted or conditioned air). Local AHJ controls permits. Atlanta metro cities all require permits.
state_notes.NC: Licensed HVAC contractor (H1-H5 license). State building code adopted statewide. Local jurisdictions control permits.
state_notes.OH: Ohio HVAC license required. Local jurisdiction controls permits. Columbus, Cleveland, Cincinnati all require permits for replacements.
state_notes.PA: Licensed HVAC contractor. Philadelphia has own permit system. Pittsburgh and surrounding areas follow IPMC.
state_notes.TN: Licensed HVAC contractor (Class A or B). Nashville Metro has own permit office. Other cities follow local jurisdiction.
state_notes.MI: Licensed mechanical contractor. Local jurisdiction controls permits. Detroit, Grand Rapids require permits for all replacements.
state_notes.MN: Licensed HVAC contractor. City of Minneapolis and St. Paul have strict licensing. Statewide permit required for all replacements.
state_notes.WA: State specialty contractor registration. Licensed HVAC contractor. Very strict enforcement on permit compliance.
state_notes.OR: Oregon CCB (Construction Contractors Board) license. Licensed HVAC contractor. Permit required statewide.
state_notes.VA: Class A or B Contractor license with HVAC specialty. Local jurisdiction controls permits. Northern VA (Fairfax, Arlington) has own offices.
state_notes.MD: HVAC technician license (HVACR). Local jurisdiction — Baltimore City has own office. MDE-related requirements for refrigerants.
state_notes.SC: Licensed HVAC contractor. Local jurisdiction. Charleston, Columbia have own permit offices.
state_notes.IN: Licensed HVAC contractor. Local jurisdiction controls permits. Indianapolis Metro has own office.
state_notes.WI: Licensed HVAC contractor (Dwelling Contractor Qualifier). Local jurisdiction. Milwaukee, Madison require permits.

electrical.governing_code: National Electrical Code (NEC / NFPA 70) — adopted by all 50 states, various editions

permit_required.rule: REQUIRED for any work that adds, modifies, or extends electrical circuits
always_required: Electrical panel upgrade (100A to 200A, or 200A to 400A)
Service entrance upgrade
Level 2 EV charger installation (new dedicated 240V circuit)
Subpanel installation
Adding new circuits to existing panel
New electrical service to an outbuilding or addition
Any work on main service equipment
Installing whole-home generator transfer switch
usually_required: Adding new outlets or switches on new circuits
Dedicated circuit for appliance (dryer, range, hot tub)
sometimes_exempt: Replacing a like-for-like outlet or switch (same circuit, no new wiring)
Replacing fixtures on existing circuits (most jurisdictions)
Minor repair work — check with AHJ
permit_required.key_rule: Any work that touches the panel, creates new circuits, or modifies service equipment requires a permit in all 50 states.

electrical.who_can_pull: Licensed Master Electrician or Electrical Contractor in most states. Some states (TX, AZ, CO) allow homeowners to pull permits for owner-occupied residences. Always verify with local AHJ.

approval_timeline.over_the_counter: Same day for standard panel upgrades in most cities
approval_timeline.plan_review: 3–10 business days for complex commercial or service upgrades
approval_timeline.noted_slow_cities: NYC (1–4 weeks), Chicago (1–2 weeks), LA (2–3 weeks)

inspections.description: Inspector checks all new wiring, conduit, junction boxes, and panel work before drywall is closed. Wire sizing, circuit breaker sizing, and grounding/bonding checked.
inspections.timing: Before closing walls or covering wiring
inspections.required_when: Any new wiring in walls or ceilings

inspections.description: Inspector verifies panel installation, proper breaker sizing, neutral/ground bus connections, labeling, and AFCI/GFCI requirements per NEC.
inspections.timing: After panel installation, before energizing

inspections.description: Confirms all circuits are working, proper GFCI protection in wet areas, smoke/CO detectors functional, panel is properly labeled.
inspections.timing: After all work complete

ev_charger_specific.permit_required: Yes — required in virtually all US jurisdictions for Level 2 (240V) EV charger installation
requirements: Dedicated 240V circuit sized at 125% of charger's continuous load (NEC 625.42)
Load calculation required — verifies panel has capacity
One-line electrical diagram showing new circuit
EVSE equipment must be UL Listed
If panel is at capacity, panel upgrade may be required
ev_charger_specific.permit_not_required_for: Using an existing NEMA 14-50 outlet that was previously permitted — no new wiring

common_mistakes: Not checking if panel has sufficient capacity before quoting EV charger — often triggers panel upgrade
Missing AFCI protection on bedrooms (required by NEC 2014+)
Improper grounding electrode system — common fail point on panel upgrades
Not labeling all breakers before final inspection
Pulling permit too late — work started before permit issued doubles fees in many cities
pro_tips: For panel upgrades, call the utility first — they need to pull the meter and may have scheduling delays (1–3 weeks)
EV charger permits are typically over-the-counter in most cities — fast to pull
NEC 2023 now requires EV-ready circuits in new construction — some jurisdictions have adopted this
If work involves utility-side of meter, a utility disconnect permit is separate from building department

state_notes.CA: NEC 2023 adopted statewide (2023 California Electrical Code). All electrical work requires C-10 licensed electrician. Strong enforcement.
state_notes.FL: Statewide licensing — EC license required. NEC 2020 statewide.
state_notes.TX: TECL (Texas Electrical Contractor License) or Master Electrician license. Local permit requirements vary by city.
state_notes.NY: NYC uses own electrical code (based on NEC). Licensed Master Electrician required for all permit work in NYC. Upstate follows NEC.
state_notes.IL: No statewide electrical license — Chicago has own strict licensing. Most suburbs require licensed electrician.
state_notes.AZ: ROC license required. NEC adoption varies by jurisdiction — Maricopa County, Tucson have own offices.
state_notes.WA: State EL01A/EL02A license. NEC 2023 statewide. Strict enforcement.
state_notes.NC: State Electrical Contractor license required. NEC 2020. Updates delayed due to hurricane response.
state_notes.CO: Denver has own electrical licensing. Colorado Division of Electrical requires Master Electrician license statewide.
state_notes.GA: Licensed electrical contractor required. Local AHJ controls permits. Atlanta metro all require permits.
state_notes.OH: Ohio Electrical License required. Local jurisdiction controls permits. Columbus has streamlined panel upgrade process.
state_notes.PA: Licensed electrical contractor. Philadelphia has own permit system and electrical inspector corps.
state_notes.TN: Licensed electrical contractor. Nashville Metro has own permit office. Tennessee Division of Fire Prevention oversight.
state_notes.MI: Licensed master electrician. Local jurisdiction. Detroit has own permit office. Expedited permits available in some cities.
state_notes.MN: Licensed electrical contractor (state license). Minneapolis and St. Paul have streamlined permit portals.
state_notes.VA: Licensed electrical contractor. DPOR licensing. Northern VA cities have own permit offices.
state_notes.MD: Licensed master electrician (state license). Baltimore City has own electrical board. Montgomery County has streamlined process.
state_notes.OR: Oregon CCB license + electrical license. BCD oversees electrical licensing. Permit required statewide.
state_notes.SC: Licensed electrical contractor. Local jurisdiction. Charleston and Columbia have own permit offices.
state_notes.IN: Licensed electrical contractor. Local jurisdiction. Indianapolis uses streamlined BEAD system.
state_notes.WI: Licensed master electrician (state). Local jurisdiction controls permits. Milwaukee, Madison have own offices.
state_notes.MO: Licensed electrical contractor. Local jurisdiction. Kansas City and St. Louis have own permit offices.
state_notes.KY: Licensed electrical contractor. Local jurisdiction. Louisville Metro has own permit office.
state_notes.LA: Licensed electrical contractor. Local jurisdiction. New Orleans has own permit system.
state_notes.OK: Licensed electrical contractor. Local jurisdiction. Oklahoma City, Tulsa have own permit offices.
state_notes.KS: Licensed electrical contractor. Local jurisdiction. Wichita, Kansas City KS have own permit offices.
state_notes.NE: Licensed electrical contractor. Local jurisdiction. Omaha has own permit office.
state_notes.IA: Licensed electrical contractor. Local jurisdiction. Des Moines has own permit office.
state_notes.UT: Licensed electrical contractor (state). Local jurisdiction. Salt Lake City has own permit office.
state_notes.NM: Licensed electrical contractor. Local jurisdiction. Albuquerque has own permit office.
state_notes.AR: Licensed electrical contractor. Local jurisdiction. Little Rock has own permit office.
state_notes.MS: Licensed electrical contractor. Local jurisdiction. Jackson has own permit office.

roofing.governing_code: International Residential Code (IRC) — Chapter 9 (Roof Assemblies). California uses California Building Code (Title 24).

permit_required.rule: REQUIRED in approximately 65–70% of US jurisdictions for a full roof replacement
always_required: Structural roof deck repair or replacement
Rafter or truss modifications
Change in roofing material type (e.g., shingles to metal)
Adding skylights or dormers
Reroofing over more than one existing layer (in jurisdictions that limit layers)
usually_required: Full roof replacement (all shingles removed and replaced)
sometimes_exempt: Minor patch repairs under 10 sq ft (varies by jurisdiction)
Replacing isolated damaged shingles without changing structure
San Diego explicitly does not require permit for 'renewal of roof covering' on CBC/CRC buildings (SDMC §129.0202)
permit_required.key_rule: If more than 25% of the roof covering is being replaced, most jurisdictions require a permit under the IRC. Always verify locally.

roofing.who_can_pull: Licensed roofing contractor (C-39 in CA, or state-equivalent). General contractor or homeowner in many states. Requirements vary widely.

approval_timeline.over_the_counter: Same day for standard re-roofs in most cities
approval_timeline.plan_review: 3–10 business days if structural work or material change involved

inspections.description: Inspector checks roof deck condition, sheathing integrity, flashing at penetrations and valleys before new material is applied.
inspections.timing: After tear-off, before new underlayment and shingles

inspections.description: Inspector verifies completed installation — shingle attachment pattern, ridge cap, flashing at all penetrations, drip edge, and compliance with local wind/fire rating requirements.
inspections.timing: After all roofing work is complete

special_requirements.CA: Title 24 cool roof requirements — certain climate zones require roofing with minimum solar reflectance. Wildfire-prone areas (WUI zones) require Class A fire-rated materials.
special_requirements.FL: Florida Building Code is strict post-Hurricane Andrew. Hurricane straps, specific nail patterns, and wind uplift ratings required. Very strictly enforced.
special_requirements.TX: Wind-rated products required in coastal counties (Galveston, Corpus Christi). No statewide license required for roofing — varies by city.
special_requirements.CO: Hail-resistant ratings (Class 4) common in Front Range cities — insurance-driven. Denver may require permit for 25%+ replacement.
special_requirements.IL: Chicago requires permit for all re-roofing. Specific nail pattern requirements per Chicago Building Code.

common_mistakes: Starting tear-off before permit is issued — inspector will not sign off and work may need to be uncovered
Not getting deck inspection before applying underlayment — common reason for failed final
Improper flashing at chimneys, skylights, and valleys — #1 cause of inspection failure
Not checking local wind zone requirements — wrong fastener pattern fails in FL, TX coastal counties
In CA: not verifying cool roof compliance before ordering materials
pro_tips: Pull the permit before tear-off — inspectors won't sign off if work is already done
In Florida, keep the permit card visibly posted on the property during work
Check if second layer is allowed — many jurisdictions limit to 2 layers maximum before full tear-off required
In hail-prone states (CO, TX, OK), document Class 4 impact resistance rating — helps homeowner with insurance

state_notes.FL: Florida Building Code is strict post-Hurricane Andrew. Hurricane straps, specific nail patterns (6d or 8d per wind zone), and wind uplift ratings strictly required. Product approval NOA numbers required on permit application.
state_notes.CA: Title 24 cool roof requirements — certain climate zones require minimum solar reflectance. Wildfire-prone areas (WUI zones) require Class A fire-rated materials. C-39 Roofing Contractor license required.
state_notes.TX: Wind-rated products required in coastal counties (Galveston, Corpus Christi, Nueces). No statewide license required for roofing — varies by city. Houston, Dallas, Austin require permits.
state_notes.CO: Hail-resistant ratings (Class 4) common in Front Range cities — insurance-driven. Denver requires permit for 25%+ replacement. Many HOAs require Class 4 in hail-prone areas.
state_notes.IL: Chicago requires permit for all re-roofing. Specific nail pattern and shingle overlap requirements per Chicago Building Code. Licensed contractor required.
state_notes.GA: Licensed general or roofing contractor. Local AHJ controls permits. Atlanta metro all require permits for full replacements.
state_notes.NC: Licensed general or roofing contractor. State-adopted IRC Chapter 9. Local jurisdiction controls permits.
state_notes.OH: Local jurisdiction controls. Most Ohio cities require permit for full roof replacement. Licensed contractor required.
state_notes.PA: Licensed contractor. Philadelphia has own permit system. Pittsburgh and surrounding areas require permits.
state_notes.TN: Licensed contractor. Nashville Metro has own permit office. Wind zones in western TN require stronger fastening.
state_notes.VA: Licensed contractor (Class A or B). Local jurisdiction. Northern VA has strict enforcement.
state_notes.MN: Ice and water shield required in climate zone 6/7 — full first 6 feet from eave required. Licensed contractor. Permit required statewide.
state_notes.WI: Ice and water shield required. Licensed contractor. Local jurisdiction controls permits.
state_notes.MI: Ice and water shield required. Licensed contractor. Local jurisdiction controls permits.
state_notes.MA: Ice and water shield required (full 24 inches past interior warm wall). Licensed contractor. Local jurisdiction.
state_notes.NY: NYC: permit required for all re-roofing. Licensed contractor required. Upstate: local jurisdiction controls.
state_notes.NJ: Licensed contractor (HIC license). Local jurisdiction. Strong enforcement in coastal areas (Cape May, Ocean County).
state_notes.SC: Licensed contractor. Hurricane zones along coast require enhanced fastening. Local jurisdiction controls.
state_notes.AZ: Low-slope (flat) roofing common. Licensed contractor (ROC). Permit required in all incorporated areas.
state_notes.NV: Licensed contractor (C-15). Clark County has own permit office. Tile roofing very common — specific requirements.
state_notes.WA: Licensed contractor (CCB). Ice and water shield required in mountain areas. Local jurisdiction controls.
state_notes.OR: Licensed contractor (CCB). Rain screen requirements in high-moisture areas. Local jurisdiction controls.
state_notes.HI: Wind and hurricane resistance critical. Licensed contractor. State adoption of IBC with significant local amendments. Permit required statewide.

plumbing.governing_code: International Plumbing Code (IPC) or Uniform Plumbing Code (UPC — CA, AZ, NV, OR, WA, ID, HI, MN). State-specific codes in some states.

permit_required.rule: REQUIRED for any work on a plumbing system beyond simple fixture replacement
always_required: Water heater replacement (gas or electric)
New plumbing rough-in (new supply and drain lines)
Moving existing plumbing supply or drain lines
Adding new plumbing fixtures (sink, toilet, shower)
Water service line replacement
Sewer lateral repair or replacement
Gas line work (new or modification)
usually_required: Replacing a water heater (virtually all jurisdictions require permit + inspection)
sometimes_exempt: Replacing a faucet on an existing supply line (no pipe work)
Replacing a toilet on existing rough-in (some jurisdictions)
permit_required.key_rule: Water heater replacement requires a permit in essentially ALL US jurisdictions. Common misconception that it doesn't.

water_heater_specific.permit_required: Yes — in virtually all US jurisdictions
inspections_required: Final inspection: verifies proper installation, T&P valve and discharge pipe, venting (gas), seismic strapping (CA, AZ, NV, WA, OR, AK, HI)
water_heater_specific.seismic_strapping_states: Required in CA, NV, AZ, OR, WA, AK, HI — and many cities in other seismic zones
common_requirements: Temperature & Pressure (T&P) relief valve with discharge pipe to within 6 inches of floor
Proper venting for gas water heaters (B-vent or PVC depending on efficiency rating)
Drip pan required where leakage could cause damage (typically 2nd floor or interior)
Expansion tank required where backflow preventer is installed (closed system)
Permit card must be posted at job site

plumbing.who_can_pull: Licensed plumber (state-specific license) in most states. Some states allow homeowners for owner-occupied single-family homes. Plumber's license required in CA, FL, TX, NY, IL, PA, and most major states.

approval_timeline.over_the_counter: Same day for water heater replacements in most cities
approval_timeline.plan_review: 3–10 business days for new rough-in or major remodel

inspections.description: Inspector checks all supply and drain lines before walls are closed. Pipe material, slope of drain lines (1/4 inch per foot minimum), proper venting, and water test.
inspections.timing: Before any plumbing work is covered

inspections.description: Verifies all fixtures are installed and functioning, water heater is properly vented and strapped, T&P valve discharge is correct, no leaks.
inspections.timing: After all plumbing work is complete

common_mistakes: Skipping the permit on water heater replacement — most common unlicensed plumbing violation
Wrong T&P valve discharge pipe material (CPVC not allowed in some jurisdictions — must be copper or galvanized)
Missing expansion tank on closed systems (required when backflow preventer is present)
Not strapping water heater in seismic zones
Drain slope too steep or too shallow — causes drainage issues that fail inspection
pro_tips: Water heater permits are usually same-day, over the counter — takes 15 minutes to pull
In CA: seismic strapping is strictly enforced — both upper and lower straps required, specific heights
Gas water heaters need B-vent inspection before firing up — confirm with inspector
In most cities, you can pull the permit online same day — no need to go to the building department

state_notes.CA: C-36 Plumbing Contractor license. Seismic strapping mandatory. Title 24 energy compliance for water heaters. UPC (Uniform Plumbing Code).
state_notes.FL: CFC (Certified Plumbing Contractor) required. IPC statewide. Very active enforcement.
state_notes.TX: Master Plumber or Journeyman Plumber under supervision. Local jurisdictions control permits.
state_notes.NY: Licensed Master Plumber required in NYC. Upstate varies by county.
state_notes.CO: Denver has strict enforcement. Outlying areas follow local jurisdictions with varying requirements.
state_notes.WA: Seismic strapping required for water heaters. Licensed plumber required. L&I oversees plumbing licensing. Permit required statewide.
state_notes.AZ: Seismic strapping required. ROC license. Permit required in all incorporated areas.
state_notes.OR: Seismic strapping required. Oregon state plumbing license required. BCD oversees all plumbing licensing.
state_notes.IL: Chicago: Licensed plumber, city-registered. Suburban Cook County: state plumbing license required. Major cities all require permits.
state_notes.GA: Licensed plumber required. Local AHJ controls permits. Atlanta metro all require permits.
state_notes.NC: Licensed plumbing contractor required. State-adopted IPC. Local jurisdiction controls permits.
state_notes.OH: Ohio Plumbing Code (OPC). Licensed plumber. Local jurisdiction controls permits. Columbus has streamlined water heater permit process.
state_notes.PA: Master Plumber required. Philadelphia and Pittsburgh have own licensing boards. Local jurisdiction controls permits.
state_notes.MI: Michigan Plumbing Code. Licensed plumber. Local jurisdiction controls permits.
state_notes.MN: Minnesota Plumbing Code. Licensed plumber for most work. Permit required statewide.
state_notes.VA: Licensed plumbing contractor. DPOR licensing. Local jurisdiction controls permits.
state_notes.MD: Licensed master plumber (state license). Baltimore City has own plumbing board. Local jurisdiction controls permits.
state_notes.TN: Licensed plumbing contractor. Nashville Metro has own permit office.
state_notes.IN: Licensed plumbing contractor. Local jurisdiction controls permits.
state_notes.WI: Licensed master plumber (state). Local jurisdiction controls permits.
state_notes.MO: Licensed plumbing contractor. Local jurisdiction. Kansas City and St. Louis have own permit offices.
state_notes.LA: Licensed plumbing contractor. Local jurisdiction. New Orleans has own permit system.
state_notes.KY: Licensed plumbing contractor. Local jurisdiction. Louisville Metro has own office.
state_notes.SC: Licensed plumbing contractor. Local jurisdiction. Charleston, Columbia have own offices.
state_notes.AK: Seismic strapping required. Licensed plumber. Permit required statewide.
state_notes.HI: Seismic strapping required. Licensed plumber. Permit required statewide. Hawaii Plumbing Code (UPC-based).
state_notes.UT: Seismic strapping required. Licensed plumber. Local jurisdiction. Salt Lake City has own office.
state_notes.ID: Seismic strapping required. Licensed plumber. Local jurisdiction.
state_notes.NM: Licensed plumber. Local jurisdiction. Albuquerque has own permit office.
state_notes.NV: Seismic strapping required. Licensed plumber (C-1D). Clark County permit office.

mini_split.governing_code: IMC (mechanical) + NEC (electrical). Refrigerant governed by EPA Section 608.

permit_required.rule: REQUIRED in virtually all jurisdictions — involves refrigerant, electrical connections, and penetrations
always_required: New mini split installation (any size)
Adding new refrigerant lines
Any electrical work (new 240V circuit for inverter compressor)
permit_required.notes: Most mini split installations require BOTH a mechanical permit AND a separate electrical permit for the new dedicated circuit.

mini_split.who_can_pull: EPA 608 certified technician required for refrigerant handling. Licensed HVAC/mechanical contractor for mechanical permit. Licensed electrician for electrical permit.

inspections.description: Checks line set sizing, insulation, penetration sealing, electrical rough-in for disconnect and circuit.
inspections.timing: Before covering line set or closing walls

inspections.description: System operational check, proper BTU sizing for space, refrigerant charge confirmed, electrical connections verified.
inspections.timing: After system is installed and running

common_mistakes: Forgetting to pull the electrical permit separately — common missed step
Improper line set sizing for run length
Not sealing penetrations through exterior walls (energy code + pest control)
Installing oversized unit — triggers failed inspection in some jurisdictions (Manual J load calc may be required)
pro_tips: Many cities allow combined mechanical + electrical permit for mini split — ask at the counter
DIY mini split installs require EPA 608 cert for refrigerant — without it, work must be permitted and inspected by licensed tech
Check local ordinance on outdoor unit placement — setbacks from property lines and windows vary

approval_timeline.over_the_counter: Same day for simple residential mini split installation (mechanical permit portion)
approval_timeline.plan_review: 1–5 business days if both mechanical and electrical permits required simultaneously. Most jurisdictions allow combined permit.
approval_timeline.note: Mini split installations typically require both mechanical AND electrical permits. Call ahead to confirm if they can be combined at your local AHJ.

state_notes.CA: C-20 Warm-Air Heating license required for mechanical portion. C-10 for electrical. Two separate permits typically required. Title 24 compliance for equipment efficiency.
state_notes.FL: CAC (Central Air Conditioning) license for mechanical. EC for electrical. Both permits required. Product approval numbers required for all equipment.
state_notes.TX: TACL license for mechanical. TECL for electrical. Local jurisdiction controls permits. Common in Houston and Austin markets.
state_notes.NY: NYC: separate mechanical and electrical permits required. Licensed Master Mechanic + Licensed Electrician. Upstate: varies by county.
state_notes.AZ: ROC license (CR-67 refrigeration). Separate permits typically required. Phoenix and Tucson have own permit offices.
state_notes.WA: CCB license. Separate mechanical and electrical permits. Refrigerant handling requires EPA 608 certification.
state_notes.CO: Denver requires both mechanical and electrical permits. Licensed contractors required for each discipline.
state_notes.GA: Licensed HVAC contractor + licensed electrician. Local AHJ controls permits.
state_notes.NC: H-series HVAC license + electrical contractor license. Local jurisdiction controls permits.
state_notes.OH: Licensed mechanical contractor + licensed electrician. Local jurisdiction controls permits.
state_notes.PA: Licensed contractor. Philadelphia requires separate mechanical and electrical permits.
state_notes.VA: Licensed contractor. DPOR licensing. Local jurisdiction controls permits.
state_notes.OR: CCB license. Both mechanical and electrical permits required. BCD oversight.
state_notes.NV: C-21 license. Clark County permit office. Separate permits for mechanical and electrical.

ev_charger.governing_code: NEC Article 625 (Electric Vehicle Power Transfer Systems). Local amendments may apply.

permit_required.rule: REQUIRED for any new dedicated circuit or hardwired EV charger installation
always_required: Installing a new 240V dedicated circuit for Level 2 charging
Hardwired EVSE (Electric Vehicle Supply Equipment) installation
Installing NEMA 14-50 outlet on new circuit
not_required: Plugging a portable Level 1 (120V) charger into existing outlet
Using an existing permitted NEMA 14-50 outlet — no new wiring

ev_charger.who_can_pull: Licensed Electrician in most states. Homeowners can pull in some states for owner-occupied homes.
key_requirements: Dedicated 240V circuit at 125% of charger's rated continuous load (NEC 625.42)
Load calculation required — panel must have capacity
If panel is full, subpanel or panel upgrade may be needed (separate permit)
One-line electrical diagram showing new circuit for permit application

inspections.description: Wire sizing, conduit installation, proper grounding verified before covering.
inspections.timing: Before closing walls or covering conduit

inspections.description: EVSE unit installed and tested, GFCI functional, circuit labeled in panel.
inspections.timing: After full installation

pro_tips: Most EV charger permits are over-the-counter same day — low complexity for inspectors
CA, WA, and OR have been pushing for pre-wired EV circuits in all new construction — check if rough-in already exists in newer homes
Many utilities offer rebates for EV charger installation — permit is required to claim them
common_mistakes: Not checking panel capacity before installing — often triggers unexpected panel upgrade
Missing GFCI protection (NEC 625.22 requirement — strictly enforced)
Incorrect wire sizing — must be 125% of charger continuous load per NEC 625.42
Not pulling a permit — common because homeowners assume plug-in = no permit
Installing EVSE that is not UL Listed — will fail inspection

approval_timeline.over_the_counter: Same day in most cities — EV charger permits are low complexity for inspectors and commonly issued over the counter
approval_timeline.note: One of the fastest permit types to obtain. Many cities have streamlined EV charger permitting due to state and utility incentive programs.

state_notes.CA: NEC 2023 (2023 California Electrical Code). C-10 licensed electrician required. LA, SF, San Diego all require permits. Many utilities offer rebates requiring permit compliance.
state_notes.FL: NEC 2020 statewide. EC license required. All cities require permit for Level 2 installation. Utility rebate programs require permit.
state_notes.TX: TECL license. Local jurisdiction. Most major TX cities require permit. Austin Energy, Oncor, and other utilities offer rebates requiring permit.
state_notes.NY: NYC: Licensed Master Electrician required. Own electrical code. Upstate: NEC-based, local jurisdiction.
state_notes.WA: NEC 2023 statewide. State EL license. Strict enforcement. Many utilities offer rebates requiring permit.
state_notes.OR: NEC 2023. CCB + electrical license. BCD oversight. Portland requires permit — streamlined process available.
state_notes.CO: Denver has own electrical licensing. NEC-based. Xcel Energy offers rebates requiring permit.
state_notes.MA: NEC 2020 with state amendments. Licensed electrician. Local jurisdiction. Eversource/National Grid rebates require permit.
state_notes.NJ: NEC 2017 adopted. Licensed electrical contractor (HIC). JCP&L, PSE&G offer rebates requiring permit.
state_notes.AZ: NEC-based local adoption. ROC license. APS, SRP offer rebates — permit required to claim.
state_notes.GA: NEC 2020. Licensed electrical contractor. Local jurisdiction. Georgia Power offers rebates requiring permit.
state_notes.NC: NEC 2020. Licensed electrical contractor. Duke Energy, Duke Progress offer rebates requiring permit.
state_notes.MN: NEC 2020. Licensed electrician (state). Xcel Energy offers rebates requiring permit.
state_notes.IL: Chicago: own electrical code. Suburbs: NEC-based. Ameren, ComEd offer rebates requiring permit.
state_notes.MI: NEC 2017. Licensed electrical contractor. Local jurisdiction. DTE, Consumers Energy rebates require permit.
state_notes.OH: NEC 2017. Licensed electrical contractor. Local jurisdiction. AEP, FirstEnergy rebates require permit.
state_notes.VA: NEC 2020. Licensed electrical contractor. Local jurisdiction. Dominion Energy rebates require permit.
state_notes.MD: NEC 2020. Licensed master electrician. BGE, Pepco offer rebates requiring permit.
state_notes.PA: NEC 2017/2020. Licensed electrical contractor. Local jurisdiction. PECO, PPL rebates require permit.

generator.governing_code: NEC Articles 445 (Generators) and 702 (Optional Standby Systems). NFPA 110 for commercial. Local gas codes for gas-powered units.
generator.permit_type: Electrical Permit + Mechanical Permit (gas units) + sometimes Building Permit

permit_required.rule: REQUIRED for standby (permanently installed) generators in virtually all jurisdictions
always_required: Standby generator permanently connected to home electrical system
Transfer switch installation (manual or automatic)
New gas line to generator (if gas-powered)
not_required: Portable generator plugged into an outlet (no permit needed)
Portable generator used with a manual transfer cord
Note: Transfer switch for ANY generator (portable or standby) requires permit if it involves panel work

generator.who_can_pull: Licensed Electrician for electrical permit. Licensed Gas Plumber or Mechanical Contractor for gas line. General or specialty contractor for concrete pad installation.

inspections.description: Gas line sizing, pressure test, proper flex connector to generator.
inspections.timing: Before backfilling trench or covering gas line

inspections.description: Transfer switch installation, connection to panel, wiring to generator.
inspections.timing: Before energizing any connections

inspections.description: Full operational test, ATS functionality, proper setback from windows/doors (typically 5 feet min), exhaust direction away from openings, proper grounding.
inspections.timing: After full installation and testing

placement_requirements: Minimum 5 feet from windows, doors, and openings (varies by jurisdiction)
Local ordinances may have noise restrictions on generator placement
NJ: New law (pending 2024) authorizes standby generators without zoning board approval in residential zones
common_mistakes: Installing generator too close to windows or HVAC return air intake — carbon monoxide hazard and code violation
Transfer switch work without permit — major code violation
Not sizing gas line for combined BTU load (if other gas appliances on same line)
Skipping the concrete pad requirement — some jurisdictions require permanent pad for standby units

state_notes.CA: Strict CARB (California Air Resources Board) rules — many generator models NOT permitted in SCAQMD (SoCal) and BAAQMD (Bay Area) air districts. Verify CARB compliance before ordering.
state_notes.TX: Post-winter storm 2021, demand surged. No statewide license for generator install — use licensed electrician for electrical portion, licensed gas plumber for gas portion.
state_notes.FL: Post-Hurricane, generators are extremely common. Strong enforcement on setback requirements and product approvals. CAC or EC license depending on scope.
state_notes.NJ: 2024 legislation streamlines permits — generators can be installed without zoning board approval in residential zones.
state_notes.NY: NYC: extensive permit process. Separate permits for electrical, mechanical (gas), and potentially environmental review. Upstate: local jurisdiction.
state_notes.GA: Licensed electrical contractor for electrical portion. Licensed gas plumber for gas. Local jurisdiction controls permits.
state_notes.NC: Licensed electrical contractor. Licensed gas contractor. Local jurisdiction. NC utilities have streamlined interconnection for backup power.
state_notes.OH: Licensed electrical contractor. Local jurisdiction controls permits. Columbus, Cleveland, Cincinnati all require permits.
state_notes.PA: Licensed contractor. Philadelphia has own permit system. Gas work requires licensed plumber/gas fitter.
state_notes.IL: Chicago: licensed electrician required. City gas permit for gas lines. Suburban: local jurisdiction.
state_notes.CO: Denver: both electrical and gas permits required. Licensed contractors for each. Some HOAs restrict generator placement.
state_notes.VA: Licensed contractor. Local jurisdiction. Northern VA: strict HOA restrictions common — verify before quoting.
state_notes.MD: Licensed master electrician + licensed gas fitter. Local jurisdiction. Baltimore City has own offices.
state_notes.WA: Licensed electrical contractor. Gas permit required for gas lines. Local jurisdiction. Strict noise ordinances in Seattle.
state_notes.OR: CCB license. Electrical and gas permits required. Local jurisdiction. Portland has strict noise ordinances.
state_notes.MN: Licensed electrical contractor + licensed gas fitter. Local jurisdiction. Cold climate = standby generators very common.
state_notes.WI: Licensed contractor. Local jurisdiction. Cold climate = high demand. Licensed gas fitter for gas lines.
state_notes.MI: Licensed contractor. Local jurisdiction. DTE natural gas areas require licensed gas contractor.
state_notes.AZ: ROC license. Air quality permit may be required in Maricopa County (MCAQD). Verify before installing diesel generators.
state_notes.SC: Licensed contractor. Hurricane zones — strong demand post-storms. Local jurisdiction.

pro_tips: Get the electrical AND gas permits at the same time — saves a second trip to the building department
Confirm setback distance from windows and doors before ordering — some HOAs and cities are stricter than code minimum
Size the gas line for peak BTU load including all other gas appliances on the same line
In CA/SoCal: check CARB compliance before ordering the generator unit — some models are not permitted in certain air districts
Transfer switch work is always permitted — never skip this permit even for a portable generator hookup

approval_timeline.over_the_counter: Electrical permit: same day to 3 days. Gas permit: same day to 3 days.
approval_timeline.plan_review: 3–7 business days if both electrical and gas permits required. Some jurisdictions require building permit for concrete pad.
approval_timeline.note: Standby generators typically need 2–3 separate permits (electrical, gas, sometimes building). Pull all at once to avoid multiple trips.

deck.governing_code: International Residential Code (IRC) Chapter 5 (Floors) and Chapter 3 (General). Section R507 specifically covers decks.

permit_required.rule: REQUIRED for most deck additions over 200 sq ft or 30 inches above grade
always_required: Attached deck over 30 inches above grade at any point
Deck over 200 square feet (most jurisdictions)
Deck attached to the structure of the house
Deck with roof, pergola, or covered structure
Deck with electrical (outlets, lighting circuits)
not_required: Freestanding (detached) deck under 200 sq ft under 30 inches above grade (most jurisdictions)
Grade-level patio (concrete or pavers) — typically no permit
Deck repairs that don't affect structural components
permit_required.irc_rule: IRC R105.2 exempts structures under 200 sq ft — BUT local amendments often lower this threshold. Always check locally.

deck.who_can_pull: Licensed General Contractor or specialty contractor. Homeowner can pull in most jurisdictions for owner-occupied single-family homes.

fee_range.high: $1,000+ (NYC, SF, LA — based on project valuation)

approval_timeline.plan_review: 5–15 business days typical — plans and footing depths must be reviewed
approval_timeline.over_the_counter: Rarely — most deck permits require plan review

what_to_submit: Site plan showing deck location relative to property lines and house
Framing plan with lumber sizes, joist spans, beam sizes
Post and footing details (depth must be below frost line)
Stair and guardrail details if deck is over 30 inches
Elevation drawings showing height above grade

inspections.description: Inspector verifies hole depth reaches below frost line, correct diameter, and placement before concrete is poured.
inspections.timing: Before pouring concrete — holes must be open for inspection

inspections.description: Inspector checks ledger board attachment, joist hangers, post sizing, beam sizing, and connection hardware.
inspections.timing: After framing is complete, before decking boards are installed

inspections.description: Guardrail height and spacing (4-inch balusters, 36-42 inch rail height), stair rise/run, all fasteners in place.
inspections.timing: After all work is complete

frost_line_depths.note: Footing depth must exceed local frost line depth

common_mistakes: Not opening footing holes for inspection before pouring concrete — major fail
Ledger board attachment not properly flashed — #1 deck failure point
Using non-approved hardware (galvanized vs stainless in coastal areas)
Guardrail balusters too wide — 4-inch sphere rule strictly enforced
Building without considering setback requirements from property line
pro_tips: Open footing holes for inspection BEFORE pouring concrete — this is the single most common deck inspection failure
Submit complete plans upfront — missing ledger board details or footing depth is the #1 reason for permit delays
In coastal or wet climates, use stainless steel hardware (hot-dipped galvanized minimum) — inspectors check this
Check setback requirements from property lines before designing — some cities require 5-10 feet from property line
Composite decking and hidden fastener systems are fine — but framing must still meet IRC R507 regardless of decking material

state_notes.CA: Title 24 energy compliance may apply to covered decks with conditioned space. C-5 (Framing) or C-10 license. Local jurisdiction. Wildfire zones: non-combustible materials may be required.
state_notes.FL: Florida Building Code requires permit for virtually all decks. Wind uplift requirements strictly enforced. Metal hardware must be hot-dipped galvanized or stainless in coastal areas.
state_notes.TX: No statewide deck code — local jurisdiction controls. Houston, Dallas, Austin all require permits for attached decks. Frost line is minimal (0-6 inches) — footings still required.
state_notes.IL: Chicago requires permit for all decks. Strict ledger attachment and flashing requirements. Suburban Cook County follows IRC with local amendments.
state_notes.GA: Licensed contractor or homeowner. Local jurisdiction controls. Atlanta metro cities require permits for all attached decks.
state_notes.NC: Licensed contractor. State-adopted IRC R507. Local jurisdiction controls permits.
state_notes.OH: Local jurisdiction controls. Most Ohio cities require permits for decks over 200 sq ft or 30 inches above grade.
state_notes.PA: Licensed contractor. Philadelphia has own permit system. Pittsburgh and surrounding areas require permits and plan review.
state_notes.TN: Licensed contractor. Nashville Metro has own permit office with online submission.
state_notes.VA: Licensed contractor (Class A or B). Local jurisdiction. Northern VA: Fairfax, Arlington have strict plan review requirements.
state_notes.MN: Frost line 42-54 inches — deep footings required. Licensed contractor. Local jurisdiction controls.
state_notes.WI: Frost line 36-48 inches. Licensed contractor. Local jurisdiction controls.
state_notes.MI: Frost line 30-42 inches. Licensed contractor. Local jurisdiction controls.
state_notes.WA: Licensed contractor (CCB). Frost line varies 12-24 inches. Local jurisdiction controls. King County has strict plan review.
state_notes.OR: Licensed contractor (CCB). Local jurisdiction. Portland Bureau of Development Services has long plan review times — budget 6-8 weeks.
state_notes.CO: Frost line 30-42 inches in Denver metro. Licensed contractor. Denver requires permit for all attached decks.
state_notes.AZ: Minimal frost concerns. Licensed contractor (ROC). Permit required in all incorporated areas. Pool fence integration often required.
state_notes.NV: Licensed contractor (C-4 Masonry or general). Clark County permit office. Pool fence integration often required.

solar.governing_code: NEC Article 690 (Solar PV Systems). IBC/IRC for structural. Local utility interconnection requirements.
solar.permit_type: Building Permit + Electrical Permit (often combined into one 'Solar Permit')

permit_required.rule: REQUIRED in all 50 states for solar PV installation
always_required: Rooftop solar PV installation of any size
Ground-mounted solar arrays
Battery storage systems connected to PV

solar.who_can_pull: Licensed Solar Contractor (C-46 in CA), Licensed Electrician, or licensed General Contractor depending on state. Some states require a PE (Professional Engineer) stamp on structural drawings for larger systems.

fee_range.SolarAPP_cities: Fee waived or $100 flat in cities using SolarAPP+ automated review

approval_timeline.SolarAPP_cities: Same day or instant approval (automated plan review)
approval_timeline.note: Over 1,000 US jurisdictions now use SolarAPP+ for instant permits on standard residential systems

what_to_submit: Site plan showing panel layout on roof
Utility interconnection application (separate — to the utility, not building dept)

pe_stamp_requirements.FL: PE stamp required for virtually all solar installations
pe_stamp_requirements.CA: PE stamp required for systems over 10kW or on complex structures
pe_stamp_requirements.TX: No statewide requirement — varies by local AHJ
pe_stamp_requirements.NJ: Structural PE stamp required for roof-mounted systems over 10kW
pe_stamp_requirements.NY: PE stamp required for commercial; residential varies

inspections.description: Conduit routing, wire sizing, grounding, and electrical rough-in verified before panels are energized.
inspections.timing: Before connecting to utility or energizing system

inspections.description: Racking attachment to roof structure, flashing around penetrations, load distribution verified.
inspections.timing: After racking is installed, before panels are placed

inspections.description: Full system operational test, proper labeling (NEC 690 requires specific disconnect labels), net meter application confirmed.
inspections.timing: After complete installation, before PTO (Permission to Operate) from utility

utility_interconnection.note: Building permit and utility interconnection are SEPARATE processes. Utility interconnection can take 2–8 weeks beyond building permit approval.
utility_interconnection.process: Submit interconnection application to utility → utility reviews → approves → installer installs → utility installs net meter → Permission to Operate (PTO) granted

common_mistakes: Assuming permit approval = can turn system on — need utility PTO separately
Incorrect labeling — NEC 690 requires specific labels on every disconnect, combiner box, and conduit
Not accounting for utility interconnection timeline in project schedule (adds 2–8 weeks)
Missing rapid shutdown requirement (NEC 690.12 — required in NEC 2017+ jurisdictions)

state_notes.CA: SB 9 and state policy streamlines solar permitting. Many cities use SolarAPP+. C-46 license or C-10 required.
state_notes.FL: PE stamp often required. Strong enforcement. Active solar market.
state_notes.TX: No statewide solar license. Varies by city. Houston, Dallas, Austin have own permit offices. Strong solar market — Tesla, Sunrun, Sunnova all active. No net metering mandate.
state_notes.AZ: Strong solar market. Maricopa County, Tucson have own permit offices. APS, SRP net metering available. ROC license required.
state_notes.NJ: Active solar market. NJBPU approvals may be required in addition to local building permit.
state_notes.NC: Active solar market. Duke Energy territories. Licensed electrical contractor required. Some cities use SolarAPP+.
state_notes.GA: Licensed electrical contractor required. Local jurisdiction. Georgia Power net metering available. Permit required statewide.
state_notes.CO: Denver and Front Range cities all require permits. Licensed contractor. Xcel Energy net metering. Some cities use SolarAPP+.
state_notes.NY: NYC: complex permit process, licensed electrician required. Upstate: varies by utility territory. NYSERDA incentives widely available.
state_notes.MA: Licensed electrical contractor. Local jurisdiction. SMART program (MA incentive) requires permit. Cape & Islands: specific requirements.
state_notes.IL: Chicago: own permit system, licensed electrician. Suburbs: local jurisdiction. ComEd net metering. Ameren net metering.
state_notes.OH: Licensed electrical contractor. Local jurisdiction. AEP, FirstEnergy net metering. Some cities use SolarAPP+.
state_notes.PA: Licensed contractor. Local jurisdiction. PECO, PPL net metering. Philadelphia has own permit office.
state_notes.MI: Licensed electrical contractor. Local jurisdiction. DTE, Consumers Energy net metering. Michigan incentive program active.
state_notes.MN: Licensed electrical contractor. Local jurisdiction. Xcel Energy has strong net metering program. Permit required statewide.
state_notes.WA: Licensed contractor. Strong solar market. PSE, Puget Sound Energy net metering. Some cities use SolarAPP+.
state_notes.OR: CCB + electrical license. Portland and other cities require permits. Pacific Power, PGE net metering.
state_notes.MD: Licensed master electrician. Local jurisdiction. BGE, Pepco net metering. SREC market active.
state_notes.VA: Licensed contractor. DPOR licensing. Dominion Energy net metering. Local jurisdiction.
state_notes.SC: Licensed contractor. Duke Energy, Dominion SC net metering. Local jurisdiction. Hurricane zones: additional structural requirements.
state_notes.HI: C-46 or C-10 license. NEM 3.0 (self-supply) now common instead of standard net metering. Very strong solar market. Hurricane ties required.
state_notes.UT: Licensed contractor. Rocky Mountain Power net metering. Local jurisdiction. Strong growth market.
state_notes.NV: Licensed contractor (C-2). NV Energy net metering. Clark County permit office. Strong solar market.

pro_tips: Start the utility interconnection application the same day you pull the permit — they run in parallel and interconnection often takes longer
In cities using SolarAPP+, permit can be instant — check if your city uses it before assuming 1-2 week wait
All disconnects, conduit, and combiner boxes require specific NEC 690 labels — prepare them before final inspection
Rapid shutdown (NEC 690.12) is required in NEC 2017+ jurisdictions — verify your inverter supports it before installing
Document PE stamp requirements before quoting — in FL and some NE states this adds 00-2,000 to project cost

water_heater.governing_code: International Plumbing Code (IPC) or Uniform Plumbing Code (UPC). NFPA 54 for gas. IRC P2801-P2804.

permit_required.rule: REQUIRED in virtually all US jurisdictions — water heater replacement is the single most common unlicensed plumbing violation
always_required: Replacing a gas water heater (tank or tankless)
Replacing an electric water heater
Installing a new water heater in a new location
Converting from tank to tankless
Converting from gas to electric or vice versa
sometimes_exempt: Some rural unincorporated counties with no building department — rare
permit_required.key_rule: Water heater replacement requires a permit in essentially ALL jurisdictions. This is the most commonly skipped permit in plumbing — and one of the most enforced.

water_heater.who_can_pull: Licensed plumber required in most states. Homeowners can pull in some states for owner-occupied single-family homes. Always verify with local AHJ.

approval_timeline.over_the_counter: Same day in most cities — one of the fastest permits to obtain
approval_timeline.plan_review: Rarely required — only if switching fuel type or adding new gas line

inspections.description: Inspector verifies: T&P (temperature & pressure) relief valve installed and discharge pipe runs to within 6 inches of floor or floor drain. Gas venting checked (B-vent or direct vent PVC). Seismic strapping verified where required. No leaks at supply connections. Drip pan present where required (upper floors, enclosed areas). Permit card posted at job site.
inspections.timing: After installation is complete, before closing any access panels
inspections.required_when: Always on permitted water heater work

common_mistakes: Skipping the permit — most common code violation for water heaters. Inspectors check during home sales and routinely find unpermitted replacements.
T&P discharge pipe wrong material — CPVC not allowed in all jurisdictions; copper or galvanized preferred
T&P discharge pipe terminates wrong — must run to within 6 inches of floor, not into a wall
Missing expansion tank when closed system exists (backflow preventer on water supply = closed system = expansion tank required)
Not strapping water heater in seismic zones — both upper and lower straps, specific heights
Gas water heater venting wrong type — high-efficiency condensing units use PVC; standard units use B-vent. Using wrong type fails inspection.
pro_tips: Water heater permits are almost always same-day, over-the-counter — takes 15 minutes online or at the counter
In CA: both upper and lower seismic straps required at specific heights (first 1/3 and last 1/3 of tank height)
Check if a closed system exists before quoting — if there's a backflow preventer, an expansion tank is required and adds to the job
Permit is required even when replacing same size, same fuel type, same location — no exceptions in most jurisdictions
Tankless conversions often require gas line upsizing — check gas supply line diameter before committing to tankless

state_notes.CA: C-36 Plumbing Contractor license. Seismic strapping is strictly enforced at specific heights. Title 24 energy code applies — minimum EF rating required. UPC.
state_notes.FL: CFC (Certified Plumbing Contractor) license required. IPC statewide. Very active enforcement on unpermitted water heater replacements.
state_notes.TX: Master or Journeyman Plumber required. Local jurisdictions control permit requirements. Houston, Dallas, Austin all require permits.
state_notes.NY: NYC: Licensed Master Plumber required. Upstate varies by county. NYC DOB requires permit for gas appliance work.
state_notes.WA: Seismic strapping required. Licensed plumber or homeowner can pull. L&I oversees plumbing licensing.
state_notes.AZ: Seismic strapping required. ROC license. Permit required in all incorporated areas.
state_notes.OR: Seismic strapping required. Oregon state plumbing license required. BCD oversees.
state_notes.CO: Denver and Front Range cities all require permits. Licensed Master Plumber.
state_notes.GA: Licensed plumber required for water heater replacement. Local AHJ controls permit fees.
state_notes.NC: Plumbing contractor license required. State-adopted IPC. Permit required statewide.
state_notes.IL: Chicago: Licensed plumber, city-registered. Suburban Cook County: state plumbing license. No permit = red-tagged at home sale.
state_notes.PA: Master Plumber required. Philadelphia and Pittsburgh have own licensing boards.
state_notes.VA: Licensed Class A or B contractor. Local jurisdiction permit required. Seismic strapping not required.
state_notes.OH: State plumbing license. Permit required statewide. Ohio Plumbing Code (OPC).
state_notes.MI: Michigan Mechanical Code. Licensed plumber. Permit required statewide.
state_notes.MN: Minnesota Plumbing Code. Licensed plumber for most work. Permit required statewide.

window_replacement.governing_code: IRC N1102 (Energy Code), IRC R308 (Glazing). IECC for energy compliance. Local wind/impact codes (FL, TX coastal, HI).

permit_required.rule: Required in most jurisdictions when replacing windows, especially for energy code compliance or structural openings
always_required: Enlarging or reducing window opening size (structural work)
Adding a new window where none existed
Window replacement in Florida and coastal/hurricane zones (impact rating required)
Commercial window replacement (virtually always)
usually_required: Residential window replacement (same size, no structural change) in most jurisdictions — for energy code compliance verification
sometimes_exempt: Like-for-like window replacement in some jurisdictions where energy code is grandfathered
Some rural/unincorporated areas with no active enforcement
permit_required.key_rule: Florida, Texas coastal, Hawaii, and all hurricane zones ALWAYS require permit and approved product numbers for impact-rated windows. Elsewhere, check locally — most require at minimum an energy compliance form.

window_replacement.who_can_pull: Licensed general contractor or window contractor. Homeowner can pull in most jurisdictions.

approval_timeline.over_the_counter: Same day for simple like-for-like replacements in most cities
approval_timeline.plan_review: 3-10 business days if structural opening change or commercial

inspections.description: Inspector checks structural rough opening size, header sizing if enlarged, flashing and moisture barrier installation.
inspections.timing: After framing, before siding or exterior finish

inspections.description: Inspector verifies: correct window product installed (matching permit specs), proper flashing and sealing, energy code compliance (U-factor and SHGC ratings on label), impact rating (FL/coastal), proper egress size for bedroom windows.
inspections.timing: After all windows are installed

common_mistakes: Installing wrong U-factor or SHGC rating for climate zone — inspector checks sticker on window frame
In FL: installing windows without Florida Product Approval (NOA) number — immediate rejection
Bedroom window egress too small — IRC requires minimum 5.7 sq ft opening, 24-inch height, 20-inch width
Not flashing properly around rough opening — #1 cause of water intrusion and failed inspection
Changing opening size without structural permit — often triggers structural plan review
pro_tips: Always pull the window label off the glass (not the frame) for inspection — inspector checks U-factor and SHGC ratings
In FL: verify Florida Product Approval number before ordering — not all national brands have FL approval
In TX coastal counties: check HVHZ (High Velocity Hurricane Zone) requirements before quoting impact windows
Bedroom egress: measure the NET clear opening of the window (not rough opening) — inspectors measure this carefully
Energy code compliance forms (COMcheck or REScheck) may be required in some jurisdictions — check at permit application

state_notes.FL: Florida Building Code strictly requires approved impact products in all wind-borne debris regions (essentially all of FL). Florida Product Approval NOA number required on permit application. Non-compliance = stop-work order.
state_notes.CA: Title 24 energy compliance required. U-factor and SHGC climate zone requirements. CSLB license for contractor.
state_notes.TX: Coastal counties (HVHZ zones) require impact-rated windows. Inland areas vary by city.
state_notes.NY: NYC has specific energy codes (NYCECC). Licensed contractor required for NYC work.
state_notes.CO: IRC energy code — climate zone 5/6 requires low U-factor windows (0.30 or below in Denver).
state_notes.WA: Washington State Energy Code (WSEC) — strict climate zone requirements.
state_notes.NC: State energy code requires U-factor 0.32 max in climate zone 4. Licensed contractor.
state_notes.GA: Energy code compliance form required. Licensed contractor.
state_notes.AZ: Low SHGC critical in hot climate (0.25 or below in Phoenix climate zone 2B).
state_notes.MN: Climate zone 6/7 — very low U-factor required (0.27 or below). Triple pane strongly recommended.
state_notes.IL: Chicago climate zone 5A — U-factor 0.30 or below required. Licensed contractor.
state_notes.OH: Climate zone 5A. Licensed contractor. Permit generally required.

fence.governing_code: IRC R105.2 (exemption thresholds). Local zoning ordinances (height limits vary widely by jurisdiction).

permit_required.rule: Varies heavily by jurisdiction — typically required when fence exceeds 6 feet in height or is in a regulated zone
always_required: Fence over 6 feet tall (most jurisdictions)
Fence in a corner lot visibility triangle (safety hazard area)
Fence over 3.5 feet in front yard setback (many cities)
Pool/spa barrier fence (REQUIRED by code everywhere — IRC R326/ISPSC Chapter 3)
Retaining wall over 4 feet tall (structural permit in most jurisdictions)
not_required: Fence under 6 feet in rear/side yard in most jurisdictions (verify locally)
Fence replacement same height, same location in many jurisdictions
permit_required.key_rule: Pool fences are ALWAYS required by code and ALWAYS need permit. For standard fences, under 6 feet in most jurisdictions is exempt — but always verify locally because zoning rules are highly variable.

fence.who_can_pull: Homeowner or licensed general contractor in virtually all jurisdictions.

approval_timeline.over_the_counter: Same day to 5 business days for most fence permits
approval_timeline.plan_review: 5-15 business days if retaining wall or pool barrier

inspections.description: Inspector checks footing depth and width before concrete is poured for structural retaining walls over 4 feet.
inspections.timing: Before pouring footing concrete

inspections.description: Inspector verifies fence height, setback from property line, gate latch direction (pool fences: self-closing, self-latching gate required), and no climbable horizontal rails within reach of children (pool barrier).
inspections.timing: After fence installation is complete

pool_fence_requirements.code: IRC R326 / ISPSC Chapter 3 / ASTM F1292
pool_fence_requirements.min_height: 48 inches (4 feet) minimum on all sides
pool_fence_requirements.gate_requirements: Self-closing, self-latching gate. Latch must be on pool side or at least 54 inches high. Gate must open outward away from pool.
pool_fence_requirements.climbability: No horizontal rails or openings within 45 inches of ground on pool side that would allow climbing
pool_fence_requirements.openings: No opening larger than 4 inches to prevent child from passing through

common_mistakes: Building in the right-of-way or easement — always pull a plot plan and verify property line before digging
Front yard fence too tall — most cities limit front yard fences to 3-4 feet for sight lines
Pool fence gate swings inward (toward pool) — code requires it swing outward, away from pool
Not calling 811 (Dig Safe) before digging post holes — required by law in all 50 states
HOA restrictions not checked — HOA rules can be stricter than city code on fence materials and height
pro_tips: Call 811 (Call Before You Dig) at least 3 business days before any post hole digging — required by law in all 50 states, free service
Pull a copy of your survey or plot plan before permitting — many cities require it to show fence location relative to property lines
Pool fences: self-closing, self-latching gate is mandatory — buy the correct hardware before inspection
Retaining walls: over 4 feet typically requires a structural permit and engineer-stamped drawings in most cities
Corner lot visibility triangles are strictly enforced — check with city before installing any fence near an intersection

state_notes.FL: Pool fence requirements strictly enforced statewide — Florida has some of the highest child drowning rates and inspectors are thorough. Florida Building Code Chapter 4 pool barriers.
state_notes.CA: Pool fencing required (Health & Safety Code 115922). Local jurisdictions have additional height and setback rules.
state_notes.TX: No statewide fence code — entirely local jurisdiction. Pool fences follow local ordinance.
state_notes.AZ: Pool fencing strictly enforced due to drowning statistics. Local ordinances vary.
state_notes.NY: NYC has specific fence regulations (NYC Building Code Section 3202). Permits required for fences over 6 feet.
state_notes.CO: Denver: permit required for fences over 6 feet. Suburban cities vary.
state_notes.IL: Chicago: permit required for all fences over 5 feet.
state_notes.GA: Local jurisdiction controls. Most GA cities require permit for fences over 6 feet.
state_notes.NC: Local jurisdiction controls. Charlotte, Raleigh require permits for fences over 6 feet.
state_notes.OH: Local jurisdiction. Columbus, Cleveland require permits for fences over 6 feet.

pool.governing_code: International Swimming Pool and Spa Code (ISPSC). IRC R326. Local health department requirements.
pool.permit_type: Building Permit + Electrical Permit + Plumbing Permit (often combined into Pool Permit)

permit_required.rule: REQUIRED for any in-ground pool installation in all 50 states. Above-ground pools over 24 inches deep also typically require permit.
always_required: In-ground pool of any size
Permanent above-ground pool over 24 inches in depth
Spa or hot tub with permanent electrical connection
Pool equipment (pump, heater, filter) electrical connections
Pool barrier fence (always required with pool permit)
sometimes_exempt: Portable hot tub plugged into existing GFCI outlet (some jurisdictions)

pool.who_can_pull: Licensed pool contractor (specialty license required in FL, CA, TX, AZ). General contractor or licensed specialty contractor in other states.

approval_timeline.plan_review: 2-6 weeks typical — pool permits require full structural, electrical, and plumbing review
approval_timeline.note: Pool permits are among the most complex residential permits — plan for 2-6 weeks minimum

what_to_submit: Site plan showing pool location, setbacks from property lines and structures
Electrical one-line diagram (pump, heater, lighting, GFCI)
Equipment specs (pump model, heater BTU, filter size)

inspections.description: Inspector verifies rebar layout, spacing, and depth before concrete shell is poured.
inspections.timing: Before gunite or shotcrete is applied

inspections.description: Conduit, bonding, and GFCI protection for all pool equipment and lights. NEC 680 bonding requirements verified.
inspections.timing: Before backfilling or covering conduit

inspections.description: All suction and return lines, main drains (anti-vortex covers required), and equipment connections.
inspections.timing: Before backfilling

inspections.description: Pool barrier fence inspected before pool is filled with water. Self-closing, self-latching gate verified. All IRC R326 / ISPSC requirements.
inspections.timing: Before pool is filled

inspections.description: Complete system test — all equipment running, GFCI working, bonding grid tested, safety equipment present, barrier complete, permits finaled.
inspections.timing: After all work complete, before pool is open for use

common_mistakes: Filling the pool before barrier fence inspection — inspector will not final without fence in place
Missing NEC 680 equipotential bonding — all metal within 5 feet of pool water must be bonded. Common electrical inspection failure.
Anti-vortex drain covers missing — required by Virginia Graeme Baker Pool and Spa Safety Act (federal law)
Setback violations from property lines or structures — verify local setbacks before excavation
No GFCI protection on all receptacles within 20 feet of pool water — NEC 680 requirement
pro_tips: Barrier fence must be in place BEFORE pool is filled — schedule fence contractor early in the project
Anti-vortex drain covers are federal law (Virginia Graeme Baker Act) — never install a single main drain without a safety cover
NEC 680 bonding is strictly checked — all ladders, rails, light fixtures, equipment, and metal within 5 feet must be bonded together
Pull all three permits (building, electrical, plumbing) at the same time if the building department allows it
In FL, AZ, and TX: verify pool contractor license type before signing contract — pool specialty license is different from general contractor

state_notes.FL: CPC (Certified Pool Contractor) license required. Strict inspection process. Pool barrier law is actively enforced. Florida Building Code Chapter 4 pool barrier requirements.
state_notes.CA: C-53 Pool Contractor license. Title 24 energy compliance for pool heaters. Anti-suction drain covers required.
state_notes.TX: No state pool contractor license — city-specific licensing. Houston, Dallas, Austin all have own permit offices. Pool fencing strictly enforced.
state_notes.AZ: ROC Dual license or CR-3 (pool) required. Maricopa County has strict pool fence enforcement due to child drowning statistics.
state_notes.NV: C-13 (Swimming Pool) license. Clark County (Las Vegas) issues pool permits through county building department.
state_notes.GA: Licensed pool contractor. Local jurisdiction controls permits. HOA approval often required.
state_notes.NC: Licensed general or specialty contractor. Local jurisdiction. Raleigh and Charlotte have full permit requirements.
state_notes.TN: Licensed contractor required. Local jurisdiction. Nashville Metro has own permit office.
state_notes.SC: Licensed general or specialty contractor. Local jurisdiction. Coastal areas may have additional requirements.

bathroom_remodel.governing_code: IRC P2700-P2901 (Plumbing). IPC. NEC Chapter 5 (Bathroom electrical). IRC R303 (Ventilation).
bathroom_remodel.permit_type: Building Permit + Plumbing Permit + Electrical Permit (varies by scope)

permit_required.rule: Depends on scope. Any work on plumbing supply/drain lines, adding electrical circuits, or structural changes requires a permit.
always_required: Adding a new bathroom where none existed
Moving plumbing supply or drain lines
Adding a new electrical circuit (GFCI outlets, exhaust fan circuit)
Structural changes (removing walls, changing floor structure)
Adding a second bathroom to a home
usually_required: Converting tub to shower (new drain, liner, or surround)
Installing new exhaust fan on new circuit
not_required: Replacing fixtures on existing rough-in (toilet, faucet, showerhead — no pipe work)
Replacing tile on existing substrate (no structural or plumbing changes)
Replacing a vanity or mirror (no plumbing or electrical changes)
permit_required.key_rule: If you're moving pipes, adding circuits, or touching walls — permit required. Cosmetic replacement of fixtures only = no permit in most jurisdictions.

bathroom_remodel.who_can_pull: General contractor or homeowner in most states. Licensed plumber for plumbing permit; licensed electrician for electrical permit in many states.

approval_timeline.over_the_counter: Same day for simple plumbing or electrical permit
approval_timeline.plan_review: 5-15 business days for full remodel with structural, plumbing, and electrical

inspections.description: Inspector checks drain slope (1/4 inch per foot minimum), vent stack connections, supply line rough-in, and water test before walls are closed.
inspections.timing: Before closing walls or covering any plumbing

inspections.description: Wire sizing, GFCI circuit locations, exhaust fan wiring, box fill calculations verified before drywall.
inspections.timing: Before drywall

inspections.description: All fixtures installed and functional, GFCI outlets tested, exhaust fan operational, no visible leaks, waterproofing complete on shower surround.
inspections.timing: After all work complete

common_mistakes: Not getting rough-in inspection before closing walls — inspector will require opening drywall
Missing GFCI outlets within 6 feet of water source — NEC 210.8(A) strictly enforced
Exhaust fan not vented to exterior (code violation to vent into attic)
Drain slope too shallow — minimum 1/4 inch per foot for all horizontal drain runs
Waterproofing not applied before tile in shower — inspector may check for cement board or Schluter system
pro_tips: GFCI: all bathroom outlets within 6 feet of a sink must be GFCI-protected — one GFCI outlet can protect downstream outlets on the same circuit
Exhaust fan must vent to the outside, not into the attic — inspector will check and this is a very common violation
If moving the toilet location, confirm drain stack location first — moving beyond 18 inches typically requires re-venting
Schluter KERDI or equivalent waterproof membrane in shower is easier to get approved than traditional felt/hot-mop
Pull plumbing and electrical permits separately if different trades doing the work — each sub needs to sign their own permit

state_notes.CA: C-36 Plumbing, C-10 Electrical. Title 24 energy code for ventilation. Strong enforcement especially in Bay Area and LA.
state_notes.FL: CFC Plumbing, EC Electrical licenses required. IPC. Florida Building Code strictly enforced.
state_notes.TX: Master Plumber, TECL Electrician. Local jurisdictions control permits. Houston, Dallas, Austin have full permit requirements.
state_notes.NY: NYC: Licensed Master Plumber + Licensed Electrician both required. DOB permit required for any work beyond cosmetic.
state_notes.IL: Chicago requires licensed electrician for all electrical work. Plumbing contractors must be city-licensed.
state_notes.CO: Denver: strong enforcement. Licensed plumber and electrician. Permit required for any supply/drain modification.
state_notes.WA: State plumbing and electrical licenses required. Permit required statewide.
state_notes.GA: Licensed plumber and electrician. Local jurisdiction permits.
state_notes.NC: Licensed contractors required. State IPC. Local jurisdiction permits.
state_notes.AZ: ROC license required. Maricopa County and Tucson have full permit offices.

shed.governing_code: IRC R105.2 (permit exemption thresholds). Local zoning for setbacks and size limits.

permit_required.rule: Depends on size. IRC R105.2 exempts structures under 200 sq ft, but local jurisdictions vary widely — some require permits for structures over 120 sq ft or over 10 feet tall.
always_required: Shed over 200 square feet in most jurisdictions
Shed with electrical (any shed with power always requires electrical permit)
Shed with plumbing
Shed on permanent foundation
Shed in HOA community (HOA approval plus permit)
sometimes_exempt: Prefab shed under 200 sq ft on skids (no permanent foundation) in most jurisdictions
Shed under 120 sq ft in some cities — verify locally
permit_required.key_rule: Under 200 sq ft on skids (no foundation) = often exempt from building permit. ANY electrical to the shed = always requires electrical permit regardless of shed size.

shed.who_can_pull: Homeowner or general contractor. Licensed electrician for any electrical work.

approval_timeline.over_the_counter: Same day to 5 business days for simple shed permits
approval_timeline.plan_review: 5-15 business days for larger or complex structures

inspections.description: For sheds on permanent foundations, inspector checks footing depth and dimensions before concrete pour.
inspections.timing: Before pouring concrete

inspections.description: Wall framing, roof structure, and sheathing verified before exterior finish.
inspections.timing: After framing, before siding or roofing

inspections.description: Setback compliance, height compliance, overall structure, electrical if applicable.
inspections.timing: After all work complete

common_mistakes: Building within setback from property line — most cities require 5-6 feet from property lines and 10+ feet from house
Adding electrical to an exempt shed without pulling electrical permit — this is a very common violation
Not checking HOA approval requirements — HOA can require removal even if city permitted it
Buying oversized prefab that exceeds city's exempt square footage limit
Not checking deed restrictions — some properties have covenants prohibiting outbuildings
pro_tips: Under 200 sq ft on skids with no electrical = likely permit-exempt in most jurisdictions, but always verify locally
Adding power to a shed? Pull the electrical permit even if the shed itself is exempt — this is always required
Call 811 before any post setting or anchor work — required by law
Check setbacks from property lines AND from your main house — both matter
HOA approval is separate from city permit — get both before breaking ground

state_notes.CA: Title 24 energy compliance applies to conditioned structures. Local jurisdictions vary widely on size thresholds.
state_notes.FL: Florida Building Code requires permit for any structure over 100 sq ft (lower than IRC). Wind load requirements apply to all structures.
state_notes.TX: Highly variable — some Texas cities have no requirements for small residential sheds.
state_notes.NY: NYC: virtually all structures require permit. Upstate: follows local jurisdiction with IRC base.
state_notes.CO: Denver: permit required for any structure over 120 sq ft. HOA approval very common in Denver suburbs.
state_notes.GA: Local jurisdiction. Most Georgia cities follow IRC 200 sq ft threshold.
state_notes.NC: Local jurisdiction. Charlotte and Raleigh require permit for structures over 144 sq ft.
state_notes.IL: Chicago: permit required for virtually all outbuildings. Suburbs: follow local code.
